what: what is cross site request forgery
why: why are hidden inputs used in forms for csrf
why do we need csrf tokens

1_beginner_explanation.summary: CSRF is an attack where a hacker tricks you into taking an action on a website you are already logged into. If you are logged into your bank, the browser automatically attaches your authentication cookies to any request going to the bank. If you visit `hacker.com`, the hacker can put an invisible form on their page that automatically submits a 'Transfer $1000' request to the bank. The bank sees your valid cookie and approves the transfer.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: XSS is a thief breaking into your house to steal your ID card. CSRF is the thief tricking you into walking into the bank yourself and handing the teller a forged withdrawal slip. You are holding your own ID card, so the bank trusts you, even though the thief handed you the slip.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: The Same-Origin Policy (SOP) prevents `hacker.com` from *reading* data from `bank.com`, but it DOES NOT prevent `hacker.com` from *sending* a POST request to `bank.com`. If the victim has an active session cookie, the browser automatically attaches it to the forged POST request.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Historically, the defense was a 'CSRF Token': a random secret string the server generates. The hacker doesn't know this string, so their forged request gets rejected by the server.

3_real_world_example.use_case: A hacker sending a phishing email with a link to their malicious website.
3_real_world_example.code:
<!-- On hacker.com, this hidden form submits automatically on page load -->
<form action="https://yourbank.com/transfer" method="POST" id="evil-form">
  <input type="hidden" name="amount" value="10000">
  <input type="hidden" name="destination" value="HackerAccount">
</form>
<script>
  // As soon as the victim visits hacker.com, it submits the form.
  // Since they are logged into yourbank.com in another tab, the browser 
  // attaches their cookies! The bank thinks the user did it.
  document.getElementById('evil-form').submit();
</script>

4_common_confusion.trap: Confusing CSRF with XSS. In XSS, the hacker's code runs on the target site (`bank.com`). In CSRF, the hacker's code runs on a completely separate, malicious site (`hacker.com`), exploiting the fact that the browser blindly sends cookies.
4_common_confusion.fix: XSS = Hacker runs code on YOUR site. CSRF = Hacker tricks user on THEIR site to send a request to YOUR site.

6_edge_case.quirk: Modern browsers largely killed CSRF by introducing the `SameSite` attribute for cookies. If a server sets `Set-Cookie: session=123; SameSite=Lax`, the browser will flat-out refuse to attach the cookie to POST requests originating from a different domain (like `hacker.com`).
